content: All code changes must strictly follow the Red-Green-Refactor cycle's 3 phases.
Express feature requirements as tests.
Intentionally write failing tests.
Verify tests fail for the right reason.
Write minimal code to pass the test.
During the Green phase, focus on working code over quality.
Prohibit excessive design or optimization during the Green phase.
During the Refactor phase, remove duplication, improve clarity, optimize performance, and apply design patterns.
Do not write production code without a failing unit test.
Do not write more of a unit test than is sufficient to fail.
Do not write more production code than is sufficient to pass the one failing unit test.
The AI's code generation order is: 1. Write test, 2. Run test and verify failure, 3. Write minimal implementation code, 4. Run test and verify pass, 5. Refactor (if needed), 6. Run entire test suite.
AI is prohibited from writing code without tests.
AI is prohibited from implementing multiple features at once.
AI is prohibited from refactoring without tests.
AI is prohibited from writing tests that don't fail initially.
Clean code first before making changes to make the change easier.

Cleanup targets include splitting long functions, extracting common functions from duplicate code, using named constants for magic numbers, using intention-revealing names for unclear variables, and clearing up complex conditionals.
Strategies for creating option value include introducing interfaces, using configurable parameters, building a plugin architecture, and utilizing feature flags.
In the analysis phase, the AI should divide features into small units, define input/output, derive test scenarios, and identify dependencies.
In the design phase, the AI should design interfaces first, identify dependency injection points, establish a test double strategy, and define module boundaries.
The test pyramid should be composed of approximately 70% unit tests, 20% integration tests, and 10% E2E tests.
Test names should follow the pattern: 'should_[expected_result]_when_[condition]'.
Code quality metric thresholds should be met: Code coverage >80%, Cyclomatic complexity <10, Code duplication rate <5%, Test execution time <5 minutes.
Conduct retrospectives on a regular cycle: Daily for TDD, Weekly for code quality, Monthly for process, and Quarterly for architecture.
For changes with high impact and high complexity, applying 'Tidy First' is mandatory.
For risky changes, split them into smaller steps.
If no tests exist for the code to be changed, the Red-Green-Refactor cycle must be strictly applied.
The priority order for AI development is: 1. Safety (tests pass), 2. Correctness (meets requirements), 3. Readability, 4. Performance (only when needed), 5. Beauty.
Automation is mandatory for test execution, code formatting, lint checking, and coverage measurement.
Automation is recommended for dependency updates, security vulnerability scanning, performance benchmarking, and documentation generation.
Before starting development, ensure requirements are broken down into testable units.
Before starting development, ensure test scenarios are identified and interface contracts are defined.
During development, write a failing test first, then implement minimal code to pass, run all tests after each change, refactor only when tests are green, and keep cycles short.
Before committing, ensure all tests pass.
Before committing, ensure code coverage meets threshold, there is no code duplication, the code is clear and expressive, and there is no commented-out code.
